intro: Your Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE) is the number of calories your body burns each day when all activity is accounted for. Knowing your TDEE is the foundation of any nutrition plan — eat below it to lose weight, at it to maintain, and above it to build muscle. Our TDEE calculator uses the Mifflin-St Jeor equation, which research shows is the most accurate BMR formula for most adults.
howItWorks: The calculator first computes your Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) — the calories burned at complete rest — using the Mifflin-St Jeor equation: (10 × weight in kg) + (6.25 × height in cm) − (5 × age) + 5 for men, or −161 for women. BMR is then multiplied by an activity multiplier (1.2 for sedentary up to 1.9 for very active) to produce your TDEE. Calorie targets for weight loss and gain are derived from TDEE adjustments of −500 and +300 calories respectively.
interpretationGuide: TDEE is an estimate — individual metabolism varies by up to ±200 calories due to genetics, gut microbiome, and measurement error. Track actual weight change over 2–3 weeks to calibrate. If you're not losing weight on a 500-calorie deficit, your true TDEE may be lower than calculated. The macro split shown (40% carbs / 30% protein / 30% fat) is a balanced general guideline; adjust to your preferences.

faqs.question: What is TDEE?
faqs.answer: TDEE stands for Total Daily Energy Expenditure — the total number of calories you burn in a day including your basal metabolic rate, exercise, and all physical activity. It's the number you compare food intake against to manage weight.

faqs.question: What is BMR?
faqs.answer: BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) is the number of calories your body burns at complete rest — just to keep organs functioning (breathing, circulation, digestion). BMR accounts for roughly 60–70% of total daily calorie burn.

faqs.question: Which BMR formula does this calculator use?
faqs.answer: This calculator uses the Mifflin-St Jeor equation, which was validated in a 2005 meta-analysis as the most accurate BMR formula for most people. Other formulas like Harris-Benedict tend to overestimate by 5–15%.

faqs.question: How many calories should I eat to lose weight?
faqs.answer: A deficit of 500 calories/day below TDEE produces roughly 0.5 kg (1 lb) of fat loss per week. Deficits beyond 1,000 calories/day are generally not recommended as they increase muscle loss and are hard to sustain. Aim for slow, steady loss.

faqs.question: How many calories to build muscle?
faqs.answer: A modest surplus of 200–500 calories above TDEE (a 'lean bulk') is optimal for muscle building while minimising fat gain. Larger surpluses ('dirty bulk') build muscle faster but add significant fat. Pair with a progressive resistance training programme.

faqs.question: Why is my TDEE different from what I calculated elsewhere?
faqs.answer: Different TDEE calculators use different BMR formulas (Mifflin-St Jeor, Harris-Benedict, Katch-McArdle) and different activity multipliers. Results typically differ by 5–10%. All are estimates — use whichever result you track consistently.

faqs.question: What activity level should I choose?
faqs.answer: Be honest — most people overestimate activity level. Sedentary: desk job, no planned exercise. Light: 1–3 days/week light exercise. Moderate: 3–5 days/week moderate exercise. Active: 6–7 days/week intense training. Very active: athlete or physical labour job.

faqs.question: Does TDEE change over time?
faqs.answer: Yes. TDEE decreases as you lose weight (smaller body = lower BMR), as you age (muscle mass declines), and with prolonged calorie restriction (metabolic adaptation). Recalculate every 5–10 kg of weight change or every few months.

faqs.question: How many grams of protein should I eat?
faqs.answer: A general guideline is 1.6–2.2g of protein per kg of bodyweight for people who train regularly. The macro split shown (30% of calories from protein) is a good starting point, but dedicated athletes often benefit from the higher end.

faqs.question: Is TDEE the same as maintenance calories?
faqs.answer: Yes — TDEE and maintenance calories are the same thing. Eating at your TDEE means your weight stays stable over time.

faqs.question: Can I use TDEE for keto or other low-carb diets?
faqs.answer: Yes. TDEE tells you your total calorie target; you can distribute those calories across any macro ratio including keto (typically 70% fat / 25% protein / 5% carbs). Total calories still determine weight change regardless of macro split.

faqs.question: What is the thermic effect of food?
faqs.answer: The thermic effect of food (TEF) is the energy cost of digesting and metabolising food — roughly 10% of total calories consumed. It's already factored into the activity multipliers used in TDEE calculations, so you don't need to add it separately.
